task: Audit the MCP server response quality: call the production API for 5 popular tools (supabase, prisma, vitest, next-auth, tailwind-css) and check if each response includes install_command, env_vars, sdk_packages, migration data, and verified combos. Report which tools have thin responses that would disappoint an AI agent.

assignments.mcp: Call the IndieStack production API (via get_tool_details MCP tool) for these 5 slugs: supabase, prisma, vitest, next-auth, tailwind-css. For each response, check for the presence and completeness of: install_command, env_vars, sdk_packages, migration data, and verified compatible tools (combos). Build a report table showing each tool × each field with ✅/⚠️/❌ ratings. Flag any tools with thin responses that would disappoint an AI agent trying to integrate them. Note specific gaps (e.g. 'missing env_vars', 'no install command', 'zero verified combos').

decomposition.reasoning: This is a pure audit/investigation task against the live MCP API — no code changes, no frontend, no deploy. The MCP/Integration department is the only one needed. They call get_tool_details for each slug, inspect the response shape, and produce a quality report. Single department, single group.

strategy_review.reasoning: This is a high-value, low-cost diagnostic of the core product. IndieStack's entire thesis is 'AI agents check us before writing code from scratch.' If those agents get thin responses — no install commands, no env vars, no SDK packages — they have nothing actionable and will stop querying. This audit directly measures whether the primary distribution channel is delivering on the value prop. It hasn't been done before (playbook shows smoke tests and code quality, never MCP response quality). It's read-only — 5 API calls and analysis, probably under $1. And the output is immediately actionable: if supabase returns zero env_vars, that's a concrete data gap to fix. With 53 users, the quality of each interaction matters more than volume.

approved_tasks.mcp: Call the IndieStack production API (via get_tool_details MCP tool) for these 5 slugs: supabase, prisma, vitest, next-auth, tailwind-css. For each response, check for the presence and completeness of: install_command, env_vars, sdk_packages, migration data, and verified compatible tools (combos). Build a report table showing each tool × each field with ✅/⚠️/❌ ratings. Flag any tools with thin responses that would disappoint an AI agent trying to integrate them. Note specific gaps (e.g. 'missing env_vars', 'no install command', 'zero verified combos').

conditions: If slugs don't resolve (e.g. 'tailwind-css' vs 'tailwindcss'), note the failed lookup as its own finding — slug discoverability is part of the agent experience
Report should include a priority-ranked list of which gaps to fix first, not just a flat table
risk_flags: Some slugs may not match exactly — this is itself useful signal about the API's fuzzy matching capability
Don't over-index on 5 tools — these are popular/well-known, so they're likely the BEST case. The long tail of 3,100 tools is probably worse
